skill.name: context7-efficient
skill.description: Fetch focused library documentation through Context7 MCP and local shell filters. The skill resolves library names, retrieves current docs, and returns concise examples, API signatures, and notes.
skill.summary: Fetch concise Context7 library documentation with local filtering.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many documentation false positives, especially markdown backticks and weak-crypto matches in prose. However, review confirmed a high-risk shell execution path in the generic MCP client and moderate risks from npx execution, networked MCP access, and unescaped JSON construction. No confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection was found, so this is not blocked, but it should not be published without remediation or a strong warning.

high_findings.title: User-Controlled Stdio Command Runs Through Shell
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: scripts/mcp-client.py accepts a --stdio command and passes it to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is legitimate MCP client functionality, but it allows arbitrary shell command execution if an untrusted command reaches the option.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence shows shell=True execution of a command sourced from CLI arguments. The feature has legitimate MCP use, but the command execution risk is confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Server Execution via npx
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the main workflow launches @upstash/context7-mcp through npx. This requires network access and executes package code from the npm ecosystem, creating supply-chain and runtime execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly execute an external npm package. The package choice appears purposeful for Context7, so this is a controlled but meaningful risk.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped User Values Embedded in JSON Parameters
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: library, topic, mode, and page values are interpolated into JSON strings before being parsed or sent to the MCP server. Malformed or crafted input can alter the request shape or cause parsing failures, although it does not directly prove shell command injection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible and the values come from positional or CLI input. Impact is limited to MCP request manipulation unless combined with other unsafe execution paths.

medium_findings.title: Temporary PID File Controls Process Termination
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: start and stop helpers store process IDs in /tmp and later kill the recorded PID. A stale or attacker-controlled PID file could terminate an unintended local process when the stop helper is run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The PID-file behavior is clear, but exploitation depends on local access and user execution of the stop script. This is a moderate operational risk.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backtick and Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: many static findings in SKILL.md and references/context7-tools.md are markdown command examples, JSON schemas, or words such as name and description. No evidence found that these markdown lines perform cryptography, reconnaissance, or Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown examples or prose rather than executable Ruby or cryptographic code. The actual executable risk is in the scripts, not these documentation blocks.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Suspicious Override Text
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE CHECK: searched the reviewed skill files for override, ignore-instructions, pre-approval, and administrator-style language. No evidence found of prompt injection text in the audited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no prompt-injection phrases in the main instruction and reference files. This does not prove absence in future generated documentation content.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Stdio Command Runs Through Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: scripts/mcp-client.py accepts a --stdio command and passes it to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is legitimate MCP client functionality, but it allows arbitrary shell command execution if an untrusted command reaches the option.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence shows shell=True execution of a command sourced from CLI arguments. The feature has legitimate MCP use, but the command execution risk is confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party MCP Server Execution via npx
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the main workflow launches @upstash/context7-mcp through npx. This requires network access and executes package code from the npm ecosystem, creating supply-chain and runtime execution risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly execute an external npm package. The package choice appears purposeful for Context7, so this is a controlled but meaningful risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unescaped User Values Embedded in JSON Parameters
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: library, topic, mode, and page values are interpolated into JSON strings before being parsed or sent to the MCP server. Malformed or crafted input can alter the request shape or cause parsing failures, although it does not directly prove shell command injection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible and the values come from positional or CLI input. Impact is limited to MCP request manipulation unless combined with other unsafe execution paths.

content.user_title: Fetch Focused Library Docs With Context7
content.value_statement: Developers lose time reading full documentation pages when they need one focused answer. This skill retrieves Context7 documentation and filters it into concise examples, signatures, and notes.
actual_capabilities: Resolves common library names to Context7-compatible library IDs.
Fetches Context7 documentation for a selected library, topic, mode, and page.
Filters documentation into code examples, API signatures, overview text, and important notes.
Supports direct library IDs for faster lookups when the user already knows the Context7 path.
Provides helper scripts for raw MCP calls, filtered documentation output, and local server management.
limitations: Requires shell access, Python 3, npx, and network access to run the Context7 MCP server.
Documentation quality depends on Context7 coverage for the requested library and topic.
Filtering can omit broader context that appears in the full documentation response.
The bundled MCP client can run stdio commands and should be used only with trusted command strings.

use_cases.title: Check API Usage During Implementation
use_cases.description: Fetch focused examples and signatures while adding a feature that uses a specific framework API.

use_cases.title: Compare Framework Patterns Quickly
use_cases.description: Retrieve concise documentation for routing, hooks, middleware, queries, or similar framework topics.

use_cases.title: Answer Library Questions With Less Context
use_cases.description: Use local filters to keep large documentation responses out of the assistant context window.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Examples
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for a known library topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use context7-efficient to fetch React useState examples in code mode. Summarize the main usage pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve a Library Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Use context7-efficient to resolve Prisma and fetch documentation about queries. Include only the most relevant examples.

prompt_templates.title: Research a Conceptual Topic
prompt_templates.scenario: The user needs explanation more than code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use context7-efficient in info mode for Next.js routing. Explain the concepts and list the most important notes.

prompt_templates.title: Explore Multiple Pages
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced lookup when the first page is not enough.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use context7-efficient to fetch page 1 and page 2 for React hooks. Compare the useful patterns and remove duplicates.

output_examples.output: A concise response with selected useState examples, recognized signatures, and important notes about state updates.
A short overview of common query patterns, followed by practical examples and warnings from the documentation.
A focused summary of routing concepts, relevant API references, and notes about when to request another page.

best_practices: Use direct Context7 library IDs when you already know the correct library path.
Start with a narrow topic, then request another page only when the result lacks needed detail.
Treat generated commands and third-party package execution as trusted-environment operations.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted command strings to the generic MCP client stdio option.
Do not rely on filtered output when you need a complete documentation audit.
Do not use broad topics when a specific API, method, or feature name is available.

faq.question: What does this skill fetch?
faq.answer: It fetches library documentation through Context7 and filters the result into concise developer reference material.

faq.question: Does it work without network access?
faq.answer: No. The Context7 MCP server and documentation retrieval require network access.

faq.question: Can I use a known Context7 library ID?
faq.answer: Yes. Direct library IDs skip the resolution step and make the lookup faster.

faq.question: Why does it use shell scripts?
faq.answer: The scripts keep large documentation responses in subprocesses and return only filtered content.

faq.question: Is the output always complete?
faq.answer: No. The skill intentionally filters output, so full documentation may contain additional details.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: Use it only in trusted environments because it runs local scripts, starts npx packages, and includes a generic stdio MCP client.
